skill.name: denario
skill.description: Denario helps AI assistants guide scientific research workflows, including dataset description, hypothesis generation, methodology planning, computational results, and LaTeX paper generation.
skill.summary: Guide multiagent scientific research workflows from data context to publication drafts.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, filesystem, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, and many weak-crypto and ransomware hits are false positives from documentation text. The skill still has medium risk because it guides users to install third-party tools, handle API credentials, run Docker containers, and execute agent-generated analysis code.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Generated Code Execution in Research Workflow
medium_findings.description: The research workflow states that results generation performs computational work and that the agent executes code for analyses. This is a legitimate capability, but it can execute unsafe generated code against project files or local data if users do not review methodology and outputs first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly says get_results executes the methodology and that Denario can write and execute code. The risk is inherent to the advertised functionality, not inferred from a variable name.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling for LLM and Cloud Providers
medium_findings.description: The skill documents OpenAI keys, Google service-account JSON files, .env files, and Docker env-file usage. This is expected for an LLM research tool, but it increases exposure risk if credentials are mounted into containers, logged, committed, or shared with generated code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly configure API keys and credential files. The same file also advises not committing secrets, so this is a real but documented operational risk rather than exfiltration evidence.

medium_findings.title: External Install and Runtime Commands
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes package installation, git clone, Docker pull/run, sudo package installation, and Google SDK setup commands. These commands are normal setup steps, but users should treat them as privileged external operations from third-party sources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible setup instructions and include privileged or containerized operations. No evidence shows command injection or malicious arguments in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer Is Risky but Contextual
low_findings.description: The static critical pipe-to-shell hit is the Google Cloud SDK installer command. It is a known vendor setup pattern, but piping remote installer output directly to a shell remains a risky practice for users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is present exactly as flagged. The context is vendor setup documentation, so it is risky guidance but not evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings appear to match ordinary documentation words such as description and methodology, not cryptographic functions or hash algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms being used by skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged lines show research documentation terms and examples, not cryptographic API calls. The files are Markdown documentation rather than implementation code.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to documentation links, local GUI access, source repository cloning, and public package or platform pages. No evidence found that the skill silently transmits data to those endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible user-facing setup and monitoring references. There is no code path in the skill files that sends project data or credentials to an untrusted endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Model-Driven Code Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow allows generated methodologies to be executed as computational analysis code, which should run only in reviewed and isolated projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both cited lines explicitly describe execution of analysis code. This is central to the tool behavior and not a scanner artifact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Files Passed to Containers
dangerous_patterns.description: Docker examples pass .env files and mount cloud credential JSON files into containers, increasing blast radius if the image or workflow is compromised.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly includes env-file use and credential volume mounting. It is a common deployment pattern but still a meaningful credential exposure risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The Google Cloud SDK installation command pipes a remote script into bash, which can execute unreviewed network content on the user system.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present. It appears to be vendor installation guidance, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Scientific research workflows often require repeated setup, analysis planning, code execution, and manuscript formatting. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Denario workflows for data-driven research automation.
actual_capabilities: Guides installation with uv, editable source installs, Docker, and LaTeX setup.
Explains OpenAI and Google Vertex AI credential configuration for Denario.
Documents the workflow from data description to idea, method, results, and paper generation.
Provides examples for climate, genomics, machine learning, time-series, and existing-results workflows.
Shows how to generate LaTeX manuscripts with APS journal formatting.
Covers GUI launch and common troubleshooting steps.
limitations: Requires the external Denario package, LLM provider access, and compatible Python tooling.
Generated research ideas, methods, results, and citations require expert review.
Computational execution may write files and run generated code in the project directory.
API usage, cloud services, Docker images, and LaTeX tools may add cost and operational risk.

use_cases.title: Explore Research Ideas from a Dataset
use_cases.description: Describe available data, tools, and domain constraints, then generate candidate hypotheses for review.

use_cases.title: Draft a Reproducible Analysis Pipeline
use_cases.description: Create a methodology, run controlled computations, and collect figures and results in a structured project folder.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Manuscript from Existing Results
use_cases.description: Provide existing data descriptions, methods, and results, then generate a formatted LaTeX paper draft.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Denario Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Denario skill to help me start a new research project. Ask for my dataset, tools, domain, and output goals before suggesting commands.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Research Ideas
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Denario to turn this dataset description into three testable research ideas. Include assumptions, required tools, and review checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Analysis Method
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Denario to draft a methodology for my selected research idea. Separate statistical tests, validation steps, figures, and execution risks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Paper Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Denario to plan an end-to-end workflow from existing results to an APS-style LaTeX paper. Include credential safety and reproducibility checks.

output_examples.input: A climate scientist asks for a research workflow using monthly temperature anomaly data.
output: A clarified data description with required columns, time range, and known limitations.
A testable warming-trend research question and a staged methodology.
A review checklist before running generated analysis code.

output_examples.input: A lab has completed results and wants a journal-style manuscript draft.
output: A paper-generation plan that maps results, figures, methods, and discussion sections.
A LaTeX output checklist for APS formatting and local compilation requirements.
A reminder to verify claims, citations, and statistical interpretation.

output_examples.input: A data scientist wants to configure Denario with OpenAI or Vertex AI.
output: Provider-specific credential setup guidance using environment variables or project-local configuration.
Safety notes about .env files, service-account JSON files, and Docker mounts.
Troubleshooting steps for missing keys, rate limits, and cloud project selection.

best_practices: Review every generated methodology before running results generation.
Use isolated virtual environments or containers with least-privilege credentials.
Commit reviewed data descriptions, methods, results, and papers after each stage.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated analysis code against sensitive data without review.
Do not commit .env files, API keys, or service-account JSON files.
Do not treat generated papers or citations as publication-ready without domain validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants guide Denario workflows for research idea generation, methodology planning, results generation, and paper drafting.

faq.question: Does it install Denario automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and guidance. You choose whether to run installation or Docker commands.

faq.question: Do I need API keys?
faq.answer: Yes. Denario requires supported LLM provider credentials, such as OpenAI keys or Google Vertex AI credentials.

faq.question: Can it generate publication-ready papers?
faq.answer: It can guide LaTeX paper generation, but all scientific claims, citations, and formatting must be reviewed by qualified humans.

faq.question: Is generated code execution safe?
faq.answer: It is a powerful feature with risk. Review methodology and code, isolate projects, and limit credential access before execution.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
